EXPOSÉ VIII
DIAGONALIZABLE GROUPSby A. Grothendieck0

blocks.html: 1. Biduality

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, which we identify as usual with a full subcategory of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}} = \mathrm{Hom}(\mathscr{C}^{\circ}, (\mathrm{Ens}))\) (cf. Exp. I). Let I be a commutative group-functor, i.e. an object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\) equipped with a commutative group structure (cf. I, 2.1).1 For every \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}(\widehat{\mathscr{C}})\), the object \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(X,I)\) is equipped with a commutative group structure, induced by that of I. For every group G in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), let \[ \mathrm{D}(G) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(G,I) \] be the subobject of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,I)\) defined, for every \(S \in \mathrm{Ob}(\mathscr{C})\), by:
(x) \(\mathrm{D}(G)(S) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G_S, I_S),\)

where \(G_S = G \times S\) and \(I_S = I \times S\) are considered as S-groups, i.e. as groups in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}_{/S}\). Then \(\mathrm{D}(G)\) is a sub-\(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(G,I)\). In this way one obtains a contravariant functor \(\mathrm{D}\) from the category of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups to the category of commutative \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups. The right-hand side of (x) can also be interpreted as the subset of \(\mathrm{Hom}(G\times S, I)\) consisting of the morphisms \(G \times S \to I\) that are "multiplicative with respect to the first argument G". Moreover, the preceding formulas hold more generally when S is an arbitrary object of \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), not necessarily coming from \(\mathscr{C}\).

If we now take for S a group in \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\), which we shall denote by \(G'\), then in the left-hand side \(\mathrm{Hom}(G', \mathrm{D}(G))\) of (x), we may distinguish the subset \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(G', \mathrm{D}(G))\) consisting of the morphisms that respect the group structures of \(G'\) and \(\mathrm{D}(G)\). It then corresponds to the subset of \(\mathrm{Hom}(G\times G', I)\) consisting of the morphisms that are multiplicative with respect to the first and with respect to the second argument, — which may be called bilinear morphisms from \(G \times G'\) to I, or pairings of G and \(G'\) with values in I. One thus finds
(xx) \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(G', \mathrm{D}(G)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{bil.}}(G \times G', I),\)
which is an isomorphism functorial in the pair \((G, G')\). As the right-hand side is symmetric in G and \(G'\), one deduces a functorial bijection:
(xxx) \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(G', \mathrm{D}(G)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(G, \mathrm{D}(G')).\)

In other words, "it amounts to the same" to give a group homomorphism \(G' \to \mathrm{D}(G)\), or a group homomorphism \(G \to \mathrm{D}(G')\), each of the two reducing in fact to the datum of a pairing \(G \times G' \to I\).
Applying this to the case where \(G' = \mathrm{D}(G)\), and to the identity homomorphism \(G' \to \mathrm{D}(G)\), one then finds a canonical homomorphism
(xxxx) \(G \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}(\mathrm{D}(G)).\)

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.0

2 We shall say that G is reflexive (relative to I) if the preceding homomorphism is an isomorphism. Note that this implies that G is commutative.
One thus sees that:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.0.1

The functor \(\mathrm{D}\) induces an anti-equivalence of the category of reflexive \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-groups with itself. In particular, if G, H are two reflexive groups, \(\mathrm{D}\) induces an isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(G,H) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(\mathrm{D}(H), \mathrm{D}(G)) \] (it even suffices that H be reflexive, as one sees from formula (xxx)).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.0.2

As usual, we shall then say that a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group G is reflexive if it is reflexive as a \(\widehat{\mathscr{C}}\)-group (without worrying about whether \(\mathrm{D}(G)\) is representable or not). One thus obtains, via \(\mathrm{D}\), an anti-equivalence of the category of reflexive \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups G such that \(\mathrm{D}(G)\) is representable, with itself.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.0.3

Let us point out, to finish these generalities, that the formation of the duals \(\mathrm{D}(G)\) commutes with base extension, which therefore transforms reflexive groups into reflexive groups.

We are interested in what follows in the case where \(\mathscr{C} = (\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\), the category of preschemes over S, and \(I = \mathbb{G}_{m,S}\), the "multiplicative group over S", cf. exposé I. For every ordinary group M, we consider the S-group \(M_S\). One sees immediately that for every group prescheme J over S, there is a canonical isomorphism (functorial in M and J, and compatible with base extension): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S, J) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, J(S)). \] Applying this to \(J = I = \mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) and to a variable \(S'\) over S, one finds a functorial isomorphism:
(1.0.4) \(\mathrm{D}(M_S)(S') \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, \mathbb{G}_m(S')).\)

One thus recovers the functor already considered in I, 4.4, also denoted \(\mathrm{D}_S(M)\), which is representable for M commutative, since \[ \mathrm{D}_S(M) = \mathrm{D}(M_S) = \mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{O}_S(M), \] where \(\mathscr{O}_S(M)\) denotes the algebra of the group M with coefficients in \(\mathscr{O}_S\). (Note moreover that in the general case \(\mathrm{D}(M)\) does not change if we replace M by M made abelian, so that nothing is lost by assuming M commutative).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

A group prescheme G over S is said to be diagonalizable if it is isomorphic to a scheme of the form \(\mathrm{D}_S(M) = \mathrm{D}(M_S) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S, \mathbb{G}_m)\) for a suitable commutative group M. We say that G is locally diagonalizable if every point of S admits an open neighborhood U such that \(G|_U\) is diagonalizable.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.2

Let \(\Gamma\) be a constant commutative group scheme over S, i.e. isomorphic to a group scheme of the form \(M_S\), where M is an ordinary commutative group. Then \(\Gamma\) is reflexive, i.e. the canonical homomorphism \[ \Gamma \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}(\mathrm{D}(\Gamma)) \] is an isomorphism.3 The diagonalizable group \(\mathrm{D}(M_S)\) is therefore also reflexive.
Taking the definitions into account, this follows from the following statement (which will be applied to a prescheme \(S'\) over S):

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3

Let \(G = \mathrm{D}(M_S)\). Then every homomorphism of S-groups \[ u : G \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_{m,S} \] is defined by a uniquely determined section of \(M_S\) over S, i.e. by a uniquely determined locally constant map from S to M.

Proof. Since by definition we have \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S} = \mathrm{GL}(1)_S = \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathscr{O}_S\text{-mod.}}(\mathscr{O}_S)\), one sees that giving a group homomorphism \(G \to \mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) is equivalent to giving on \(\mathscr{O}_S\) a structure of G-\(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module, compatible with the natural \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module structure on \(\mathscr{O}_S\) (cf. I, 4.7). By I, 4.7.3, this also amounts to giving a grading of type M on \(\mathscr{O}_S\), i.e. a decomposition of \(\mathscr{O}_S\) into a direct sum of modules \(\mathscr{L}_m\) (\(m \in M\)). Now it is well known that a direct factor of a locally free module of finite type is locally free of finite type, so each \(\mathscr{L}_m\) is, in the neighborhood of each point of S, either zero or free of rank 1, and in that case identical to \(\mathscr{O}_S\) in that neighborhood. Let \(S_m\) be the open subset of S consisting of the points where the second alternative occurs. Expressing that \(\mathscr{O}_S\) is the direct sum of the \(\mathscr{L}_m\), one sees that the union of the \(S_m\) is S, and that the \(S_m\) are pairwise disjoint. Hence giving a group homomorphism \(G \to \mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) is equivalent to giving a decomposition of S as a union of pairwise disjoint open subsets \(S_m\) (\(m \in M\)), i.e. to giving a locally constant map from S to M. This establishes 1.3 and hence 1.2.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4

A diagonalizable group is reflexive; the same therefore holds for a locally diagonalizable group. If M, N are two ordinary commutative groups, the natural homomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S, N_S) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{D}(N_S), \mathrm{D}(M_S)) \] is bijective.4
The preceding isomorphism being compatible with base extension, one deduces from it an isomorphism of S-group functors:
(1) \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S, N_S) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{D}(N_S), \mathrm{D}(M_S)). \]

For every S-prescheme T, we have \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S, N_S)(T) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, \Gamma(N_T/T))\), and, by I 1.8, \(\Gamma(N_T/T)\) is the abelian group of locally constant maps \(T \to N\). On the other hand, let \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,N)_S\) be the constant S-group associated with the ordinary abelian group \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,N)\). There is an obvious homomorphism of commutative S-group functors:
(2) \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,N)_S \xrightarrow{\ \theta\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(M_S, N_S), \]

which is always a monomorphism. Moreover, it is an isomorphism if M is of finite type.5 From the foregoing one deduces point a) of the following corollary; point b) follows from it by the descent results "recalled" in 1.7.6

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5

a) Let M, N be two ordinary commutative groups, with M of finite type; then we have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M,N)_S \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{D}(N_S), \mathrm{D}(M_S))\ ; \] consequently \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{D}(N_S), \mathrm{D}(M_S))\) is representable.

b) More generally, if G, H are locally diagonalizable, with H of finite type, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(G,H)\) is representable.7
From 1.5 one concludes:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.6

Under the conditions of 1.5, if S is connected, we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{D}_S(N), \mathrm{D}_S(M)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, N) \] and \[ \mathrm{Isom}_{S\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{D}_S(N), \mathrm{D}_S(M)) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Isom}_{\mathrm{gr.}}(M, N). \]
1.7. Descent of representability.
8 In this paragraph we "recall" some descent results, which will be used frequently in the sequel.

blocks.title: Scholie
blocks.html:
Scholium 1.7.1

Let S be a prescheme and X, Y, T some S-preschemes. If \((T_i)\) is an open covering of T, and if we set \(T_{ij} = T_i \cap T_j = T_i \times_T T_j\), then, since giving a morphism of T-preschemes \(X_T \to Y_T\) is local on T, we have an exact sequence of sets: \[ (1) \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_T(X_T, Y_T) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}_{T_i}(X_{T_i}, Y_{T_i}) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} \mathrm{Hom}_{T_{ij}}(X_{T_{ij}}, Y_{T_{ij}}) \] i.e. \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)\) is a local S-functor, that is, a sheaf on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\) equipped with the Zariski topology.

More generally, by IV 4.5.13, \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y)\) is a sheaf on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\) for every topology coarser than the canonical topology, for example for the (fpqc) topology.

If G, H are S-group preschemes, one deduces that the subfunctor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{S\text{-gr.}}(X,Y)\) is a sheaf for the (fpqc) topology (hence a fortiori a local functor).

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7.2

9 Let F be a local S-functor.

(i) Suppose there exists an open covering \((S_i)\) of S such that the restriction \(F_i = F \times_S S_i\) of F to each \(S_i\) is representable by an \(S_i\)-prescheme \(X_i\). Then F is representable by an S-prescheme X.

(ii) Suppose that F is an (fpqc) sheaf and that there exists a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(S' \to S\) such that the restriction \(F' = F \times_S S'\) of F is representable by an \(S'\)-prescheme \(X'\). Then \(X'\) is equipped with a descent datum (cf. IV 2.1) relative to \(S' \to S\).

If moreover this descent datum is effective (which is the case if \(X'\) is affine over \(S'\)), then F is representable by an S-prescheme X.

Proof. (i) It follows from the hypothesis that \(X_i \times_S S_j\) and \(X_j \times_S S_i\) both represent the restriction of F to \(S_{ij} = S_i \times_S S_j\); hence, by the Yoneda lemma, there exists a unique isomorphism of \(S_{ij}\)-preschemes \[ c_{ji} : X_i \times_S S_j \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X_j \times_S S_i\ ; \] one then has isomorphisms of preschemas over \(S_{ijk} = S_i \times_S S_j \times S_k\): \[ \xymatrix{ X_i \times_S S_j \times S_k \ar[r]^-{c_{ji}\times\mathrm{id}_{S_k}} \ar@{=}[d] & X_j \times_S S_i \times_S S_k \ar@{=}[r] & X_j \times_S S_k \times_S S_i \ar[d]^-{c_{kj}\times\mathrm{id}_{S_i}} \\ X_i \times_S S_k \times_S S_j \ar[rr]_-{c_{ki}\times\mathrm{id}_{S_j}} & & X_k \times_S S_i \times_S S_j \ar@{=}[r] & X_k \times_S S_j \times_S S_i } \] and since all these objects represent the restriction of F to \(S_{ijk}\), this diagram is commutative, i.e. the \(c_{ji}\) satisfy the usual cocycle relation \(c_{kj} \circ c_{ji} = c_{ki}\). It follows that the \(X_i\) glue together into an S-prescheme X such that \(X \times_S S_i = X_i\) for every i. For every Y over \(S_i\), we therefore have
(*) \(\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{Y}) = \mathrm{F}_i(\mathrm{Y}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S_i}(\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{X} \times_S S_i) = \mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{X}) = h_{\mathrm{X}}(\mathrm{Y}).\)

Then, for arbitrary Y → S, the \(Y_i = Y \times_S S_i\) form an open covering of Y; set \(Y_{ij} = Y_i \times_Y Y_j = Y \times_S S_{ij}\). Since F (resp. \(h_X\)) is a local functor by hypothesis (resp. since the Zariski topology is coarser than the canonical topology), F(Y) and \(h_X(Y)\) are both identified, taking (*) into account, with the kernel of the double arrow: \[ \xymatrix{ \prod_i F(Y_i) \ar@{=}[d] \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \prod_{i,j} F(Y_{ij}) \ar@{=}[d] \\ \prod_i h_X(Y_i) \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & \prod_{i,j} h_X(Y_{ij}) } \] This proves (i).

(ii) It follows from the hypothesis that \(F''_1 = F' \times_{S'} S''_1\) (where \(S''_1 = S'' = S' \times_S S'\) considered as an \(S'\)-prescheme via the first projection) is represented by \(X''_1 = X' \times_{S'} S''_1\); similarly, \(F''_2 = F' \times_{S'} S''_2\) is represented by \(X''_2 = X' \times_{S'} S''_2\). Now \(F''_1 = F \times_S S'' = F''_2\), so there exists a (unique) \(S''\)-isomorphism \(c : X''_1 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X''_2\); then, denoting by \(q_i\) (resp. \(p_{ji}\)) the projection of \(S''' = S' \times_S S' \times_S S'\) onto the i-th factor (resp. onto the factors i and j), \(X'''_i = X' \times_{S'} S'''_i\) (where \(S'''_i = S'''\) considered as an \(S'\)-prescheme via \(q_i\)), and by \(p_{ji}^*(c) : X'''_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } X'''_j\) the isomorphism of \(S'''\)-preschemes deduced from c by base change, one obtains a diagram of isomorphisms of \(S'''\)-preschemes: \[ \xymatrix{ X'''_1 \ar[rr]^-{p_{21}^*(c)} \ar[dr]_-{p_{31}^*(c)} & & X'''_2 \ar[dl]^-{p_{32}^*(c)} \\ & X'''_3 & } \] and since all these objects represent the restriction of F to \(S'''\), this diagram is commutative, i.e. we have the usual cocycle relation \(p_{32}^*(c) \circ p_{21}^*(c) = p_{31}^*(c)\), i.e. c is a descent datum on \(X'\) relative to \(S' \to S\) (cf. IV 2.1).

Suppose moreover that this descent datum is effective, i.e. that there exists an S-prescheme X such that \(X' \simeq X \times_S S'\) (by SGA 1, VIII 2.1, this is the case if \(X'\) is affine over \(S'\)10). Then, for every Y → \(S'\), we have
(**) \(\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{Y}) = \mathrm{F}'(\mathrm{Y}) = \mathrm{Hom}_{S'}(\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{X} \times_S S') = \mathrm{Hom}_S(\mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{X}) = h_{\mathrm{X}}(\mathrm{Y}).\)

Then, for arbitrary Y → S, set \(Y' = Y \times_S S'\) and \(Y'' = Y' \times_Y Y' \simeq Y \times_S S''\). Then \(Y' \to Y\) is, like \(S' \to S\), faithfully flat and quasi-compact, hence an \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective epimorphism (where \(\mathscr{M}\) = the family of faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms), i.e. the equivalence relation \(Y' \times_Y Y' \rightrightarrows Y'\) is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective and has quotient Y. Since F (resp. \(h_X\)) is an (fpqc) sheaf by hypothesis (resp. since the (fpqc) topology is coarser than the canonical topology), F(Y) and \(h_X(Y)\) are both identified, taking (**) into account, with the kernel of the double arrow: \[ \xymatrix{ F(Y') \ar@{=}[d] \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & F(Y' \times_Y Y') \ar@{=}[d] \\ h_X(Y') \ar@<0.5ex>[r] \ar@<-0.5ex>[r] & h_X(Y' \times_Y Y') } \] This proves (ii).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.7.3

Let F be an (fpqc) sheaf on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\). Suppose there exists an open covering \((S_i)\) of S and, for each i, a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(S'_i \to S_i\) such that the restriction \(F'_i = F \times_S S'_i\) is representable by an \(S'_i\)-prescheme \(X'_i\) affine over \(S'_i\). Then F is representable by an S-prescheme X affine over S (such that \(X \times_S S'_i = X'_i\) for every i).

If moreover each \(X'_i \to S'_i\) is a closed immersion (resp. a finite étale morphism), then the same holds for X → S.

The first assertion follows from 1.7.2. For the second, it suffices to check that each morphism \(X \times_S S_i \to S_i\) is a closed immersion (resp. finite and étale), which follows from EGA IV2, 2.7.1 (resp. and IV4, 17.7.3).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 1.7.4

Assertion 1.5 (b) follows, as announced, from 1.7.1 and 1.7.2 (i).

blocks.html: 2. Scheme-theoretic properties of diagonalizable groups
They are summarized in the following

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

Let S be a non-empty prescheme, M an ordinary commutative group, \(G = \mathrm{D}(M_S)\) the diagonalizable S-group defined by M. The following hold:

a) G is faithfully flat over S, and affine over S (a fortiori quasi-compact over S).

b) M of finite type \(\iff\) G of finite type over S \(\iff\) G of finite presentation over S.

c) M finite \(\iff\) G finite over S \(\iff\) G of finite type over S and killed by an integer \(n>0\). In that case \(\deg(G/S) = \mathrm{Card}(M)\).

c′) M a torsion group \(\iff\) G integral over S.

d) \(M = 0 \iff\) G = the unit S-group.

e) M of finite type, and the order of its torsion subgroup is prime to the residue characteristics of S \(\iff\) G is smooth over S.

The verification of a) through d) is trivial, and left to the reader. Let us prove e). If G is smooth over S, it is locally of finite presentation over S, hence of finite presentation over S since it is affine over S, hence M is of finite type. So we may already assume M of finite type, hence G of finite presentation over S. Then11 G is smooth over S if and only if its geometric fibers are, which reduces us to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field k. Writing \(M = T \times L\), with T the torsion subgroup and L free, \(L \simeq \mathbb{Z}^r\), we shall have \(\mathrm{D}(M) = \mathrm{D}(T) \times \mathrm{D}(L)\), where \(\mathrm{D}(L) \simeq \mathbb{G}_m^r\) is smooth over k. Hence \(G = \mathrm{D}(M)\) is smooth over k if and only if \(\mathrm{D}(T)\) is, which means, since \(\mathrm{D}(T)\) is finite over k of degree equal to the order n of T, that \(\mathrm{D}(T)(k)\) has n elements. Now T is isomorphic to a sum of groups \(\mathbb{Z}/n_i\mathbb{Z}\), n being the product of the \(n_i\), so \(\mathrm{D}(T)\) is the product of the \(\mathrm{D}(\mathbb{Z}/n_i\mathbb{Z}) = \boldsymbol{\mu}_{n_i}\) (the group scheme of \(n_i\)-th roots of unity), hence \[ \mathrm{card}(\mathrm{D}(T)(k)) = \prod_i \mathrm{card}\ \boldsymbol{\mu}_{n_i}(k) \] where \(\mathrm{card}\ \boldsymbol{\mu}_{n_i}(k) = \)(the number of \(n_i\)-th roots of unity in k) \(\leqslant n_i\), equality being attained if and only if \(n_i\) is prime to the characteristic p of k. Hence we have \(\mathrm{card}(\mathrm{D}(T)(k)) = n\) (where \(n = \prod_i n_i\)) if and only if all the \(n_i\) are prime to p, i.e. if and only if n is prime to p. Q.E.D.

blocks.html: 3. Exactness properties of the functor \(\mathrm{D}_S\)

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

Let S be a prescheme, and \[ 0 \longrightarrow M' \xrightarrow{\ u\ } M \xrightarrow{\ v\ } M'' \longrightarrow 0 \] an exact sequence of ordinary commutative groups. Consider the sequence of transposed homomorphisms: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}_S(M'') \xrightarrow{\ v^t\ } \mathrm{D}_S(M) \xrightarrow{\ u^t\ } \mathrm{D}_S(M') \longrightarrow 0. \] (i) \(v^t\) induces an isomorphism of \(\mathrm{D}_S(M'')\) with the kernel of \(u^t\), and \(u^t\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact.

(ii) 12 \(\mathrm{D}_S(M')\) represents the (fpqc) quotient sheaf \(\mathrm{D}_S(M)/\mathrm{D}_S(M'')\).

Let us denote by \(\mathscr{M}\) the family of faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms. First, (ii) follows from (i) (cf. IV, 4.6.5.1). Indeed, the equivalence relation in \(\mathrm{D}_S(M)\) defined by \(u^t\) is the same as the one defined by the subgroup \(\mathrm{Ker}(u^t) = \mathrm{D}_S(M'')\); since \(u^t \in \mathscr{M}\), this equivalence relation is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective (cf. IV, 3.3.2.1), and hence \(\mathrm{D}_S(M')\) represents the quotient sheaf for the (fpqc) topology (cf. IV, 4.6.5).

The first assertion of (i) is a trivial consequence of the definition of the functors \(\mathrm{D}_S(-)\); more generally, for every exact sequence \[ M' \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow M'' \longrightarrow 0 \] (without a zero on the left), we shall have an exact transposed sequence: \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}_S(M'') \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}_S(M) \longrightarrow \mathrm{D}_S(M'). \] (This is valid more generally in the context of the beginning of No. 1). On the other hand, since \(\mathrm{D}_S(M)\) and \(\mathrm{D}_S(M')\) are affine over S, \(u^t\) is necessarily an affine morphism, a fortiori quasi-compact (this whatever the homomorphism \(u : M' \to M\)). The second assertion of (i) will therefore follow from point a) in the following

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Let S be a non-empty prescheme, \(u : M' \to M\) a homomorphism of ordinary commutative groups, \(u^t : G \to G'\) the transposed homomorphism. Then:

a) For u to be a monomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u^t\) be faithfully flat.

b) For u to be an epimorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(u^t\) be a monomorphism (and then \(u^t\) is even a closed immersion).

To prove a), note that if u is a monomorphism, then \(\mathscr{O}_S(M)\) is a module over \(\mathscr{O}_S(M')\) admitting a non-empty basis (namely, the system of sections defined by any system of representatives of M modulo M′), a fortiori it is faithfully flat. Conversely, if this is the case, then \(u^t : \mathscr{O}_S(M') \to \mathscr{O}_S(M)\) is injective, which (for \(S \neq \varnothing\)) implies that \(u : M' \to M\) is injective.

To prove b), note that if u is an epimorphism, then \(\mathscr{O}_S(M') \to \mathscr{O}_S(M)\) is surjective, hence \(u^t\) is a closed immersion and a fortiori a monomorphism. Conversely, if this is the case, then \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u^t\) = the unit group; now, setting \(M'' = \mathrm{Coker}\, u\), we have seen that \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u^t \simeq \mathrm{D}_S(M'')\), so by 2.1 d) we have \(M'' = 0\), hence u is an epimorphism.
From 3.1 one concludes in the usual way:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let \(M' \xrightarrow{\ u\ } M \xrightarrow{\ v\ } M''\) be an exact sequence of ordinary commutative groups, and consider the transposed sequence \[ G'' \xrightarrow{\ v^t\ } G \xrightarrow{\ u^t\ } G'. \] Then \(v^t\) induces a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism from \(G''\) to \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u^t\), and the latter is a diagonalizable group isomorphic to \(\mathrm{D}_S(v(M)) = \mathrm{D}_S(\mathrm{Coker}\, u)\).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Let S be a prescheme, \(u : G \to H\) a homomorphism of locally diagonalizable S-group preschemes, with H of finite type over S. Set \(G' = \mathrm{Ker}\, u\). Then:

a) \(G'\) is locally diagonalizable, and it is of finite type over S if G is.

b) The quotient \(G/G'\) "exists"; more precisely, the equivalence relation defined by \(G'\) in G is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective (where \(\mathscr{M}\) = the set of faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms, cf. IV, 3.4). Moreover \(G/G'\) is locally diagonalizable, of finite type over S.

c) The homomorphism \(u : G \to H\) factors uniquely as \[ G \xrightarrow{\ v\ } G/G' \xrightarrow{\ w\ } H, \] where v is the canonical homomorphism (so v is faithfully flat and quasi-compact). Moreover w is a closed immersion, and a fortiori a monomorphism.

Finally, the quotient \(H' = H/\mathrm{Im}\, w = \mathrm{Coker}\, w = \mathrm{Coker}\, u\) exists; more precisely, the equivalence relation defined by \(G/G'\) in H is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective, and \(H'\) is of finite type over S.

The first assertion of c) is a consequence of b), by definition of the quotient \(G/G'\) (cf. IV, 3.2.3).13 Let us show that the (fpqc) quotient sheaf \(\widetilde{G}/\widetilde{G'}\) is representable. This can be checked locally on S, as can all the other assertions; we may therefore assume G and H diagonalizable, of the form \(\mathrm{D}_S(M)\) and \(\mathrm{D}_S(N)\).

Since H is of finite type over S, N is of finite type, by 2.1 b); hence, by 1.5, u is defined by a homomorphism \(u' : N \to M\). Then, by virtue of 3.1 and 3.2, \(G'\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{D}_S(\mathrm{Coker}\, u')\) and \(\widetilde{G}/\widetilde{G'}\) is representable by \(\mathrm{D}_S(\mathrm{Im}\, u')\); moreover, considering the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ker}\, u' \longrightarrow N \xrightarrow{\ w'\ } \mathrm{Im}\, u' \longrightarrow 0, \] one obtains that w is a closed immersion, and that the quotient \(H' = H/\mathrm{Im}\, w\) is \(\mathrm{D}_S(\mathrm{Ker}\, u')\); the latter is of finite type over S since N, and hence \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u'\), is of finite type.
Remarks. — The quotient existence result 3.4 will be substantially generalized in No. 5.

On the other hand, note that in the present No. and the preceding one, the hypothesis \(S \neq \varnothing\) only intervened to ensure the validity of certain converses, allowing one to deduce from certain hypotheses on diagonalizable S-groups properties of the corresponding ordinary groups. The results in the "direct" sense are valid without restriction on S, and the proofs given here apply in the general case.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let G be a diagonalizable group prescheme over S, and let n be an integer \(\neq 0\). Then the subgroup \({}_n G\) of G, the kernel of the homomorphism \(n\cdot\mathrm{id}_G : G \to G\), is integral over S, and finite over S if G is of finite type over S.
Indeed, if \(G = \mathrm{D}_S(M)\), then \({}_n G = \mathrm{D}_S(M/nM)\) by virtue of 3.1, and we conclude by 2.1 b), c), c′).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: What follows has been added, and the proof has been expanded accordingly.
N.D.E.: The original has been expanded in what follows.

blocks.html: 4. Torsors under a diagonalizable group

Let S be a prescheme, and \(G = \mathrm{D}_S(M)\) a diagonalizable group over S. We propose to determine the G-torsors (or principal homogeneous G-bundles) over S, in the sense of the "faithfully flat quasi-compact topology", cf. Exp. IV, 5.1. Recall that a prescheme P over S, with group of operators G, is called a torsor or principal homogeneous if every point of S admits an open neighborhood U and a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism \(S' \to U\), such that \(P' = P \times_S S'\) is a bundle with operators isomorphic to \(G' = G \times_S S'\) (acting on itself by right translations). Since G is affine over S, it follows by SGA 1, VIII 5.6 that P is necessarily affine over S. Note also that since G is itself faithfully flat and quasi-compact over S, P is principal homogeneous under G if and only if it is "formally principal homogeneous", and if it is moreover faithfully flat and quasi-compact over S (cf. IV, 5.1.6).

Recall on the other hand (Exp. I, 4.7.3) that giving an S-prescheme P affine over S with group of operators \(G = \mathrm{D}_S(M)\) amounts to giving a commutative quasi-coherent algebra graded of type M over S, i.e. a quasi-coherent algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) over S, equipped with a direct sum decomposition (as a module): \[ \mathscr{A} = \coprod_{m \in M} \mathscr{A}_m, \] with \[ \mathscr{A}_m \cdot \mathscr{A}_{m'} \subseteq \mathscr{A}_{m+m'} \qquad \text{for} \quad m, m' \in M. \] This being said, the answer to the problem posed above is given by the following

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.1

For the prescheme P with group of operators \(G = \mathrm{D}_S(M)\), defined by the algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) graded of type M, to be a principal homogeneous bundle under G, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathscr{A}\) satisfy the following conditions:

a) For every \(m \in M\), \(\mathscr{A}_m\) is an invertible module over S.

b) For \(m, m' \in M\), the homomorphism \[ \mathscr{A}_m \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_S} \mathscr{A}_{m'} \longrightarrow \mathscr{A}_{m+m'} \] induced by the multiplication in \(\mathscr{A}\), is an isomorphism.

The necessity of the conditions is immediate by descent, for they are satisfied in the case where P is the trivial principal homogeneous bundle, i.e. \(\mathscr{A} = \mathscr{O}_S(M)\). For the sufficiency, note that a) already implies that P is faithfully flat over S; it is in any case quasi-compact over S (being affine over S), so it remains to check that it is formally principal homogeneous under G, i.e. that the well-known homomorphism \[ P \underset{S}{\times} G \longrightarrow P \underset{S}{\times} P \] is an isomorphism. Now on the affine algebras, this homomorphism is made explicit as the homomorphism \[ \mathscr{A} \otimes \mathscr{A} \longrightarrow \mathscr{A}(\mathrm{M}) = \mathscr{A} \otimes \mathscr{O}_S(M) \] which in bidegree \((m,n)\) (where \(m,n \in M\)) is given by \[ x_m \otimes y_n \longmapsto x_m y_n \otimes e_n. \] From the point of view of degrees, this homomorphism is compatible with the homomorphism \(M \times M \to M \times M\) given by \((m,n) \longmapsto (m+n, n)\), which is an isomorphism. This shows that b) expresses precisely (independently of a)) that P is formally principal homogeneous, and establishes 4.1.
Note also that one obtains, by faithfully flat descent:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.2

The conditions of 4.1 imply that the homomorphism \[ \mathscr{O}_S \longrightarrow \mathscr{A}_0 \] is an isomorphism.

If for example \(M = \mathbb{Z}\), then under the conditions of 4.1 one sees that \(\mathscr{A}\) is essentially known once one knows \(\mathscr{A}_1 = \mathscr{L}\), namely \[ \mathscr{A} \simeq \coprod_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \mathscr{L}^{\otimes n} \] (an isomorphism of graded algebras). One thus recovers the well-known result:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3

There is an equivalence between the category of principal homogeneous bundles P over S with group \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\), and the category of invertible modules \(\mathscr{L}\) over S (taking as morphisms, to define both categories, the isomorphisms for the structures involved). One obtains two functors quasi-inverse to each other by associating with every P the degree 1 component of its \(\mathbb{Z}\)-graded affine algebra, and by associating with every \(\mathscr{L}\) the spectrum of the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-graded algebra \(\coprod_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} \mathscr{L}^{\otimes n}\).
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

The group of classes of principal homogeneous bundles over S with group \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) is isomorphic to the group \(\mathrm{Pic}(S)\) of classes of invertible modules over S, i.e. to \(\mathrm{H}^1(S, \mathscr{O}_S^{\times})\).

Taking into account that \(\mathbb{G}_{m,S}\) is the scheme of automorphisms of the module \(\mathscr{O}_S\), one sees that 4.4 is equivalent to the following statement, which is one of the variants of Hilbert's "Theorem 90".

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5

Every principal homogeneous bundle over S with group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) is locally trivial (in the sense of the Zariski topology).

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 4.5.1

Note that the preceding statement is no longer true in general for a group such as \(\boldsymbol{\mu}_n\), or for a "twisted form" of \(\mathbb{G}_m\); for example the unique twisted form of \(\mathbb{G}_m\) over the field \(\mathbb{R}\) of real numbers gives a 1-cohomology group equal to \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\).14
In the next No. we shall need the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6

Under the conditions of 4.1, conditions a) and b) are equivalent to the following conditions:

a′) \(\mathscr{O}_S \to \mathscr{A}_0\) is an isomorphism.

b′) For every m in M (it suffices: in a system of generators of M), we have: \[ \mathscr{A}_m \cdot \mathscr{A}_{-m} = \mathscr{A}_0. \]
The necessity being obvious, taking 4.2 into account15, we shall reduce to proving the following

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7

Let \(A = \coprod_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} A_n\) be a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-graded ring, such that \[ A_1 \cdot A_{-1} = A_0. \] Then the \(A_n\) are invertible \(A_0\)-modules, and for \(n, n' \in \mathbb{Z}\), the homomorphism \[ A_n \otimes_{A_0} A_{n'} \longrightarrow A_{n+n'} \] induced by the multiplication in A, is an isomorphism.
By hypothesis, there exist \(f_i \in A_1\), \(g_i \in A_{-1}\), such that
(*) \(\displaystyle\sum_i f_i g_i = 1.\)

Since the conclusion to be established is local on \(\mathrm{Spec}(A_0)\),16 and since, by (*), \(\mathrm{Spec}(A_0)\) is covered by the affine open sets \(D(f_i g_i)\), we are reduced to the case where there exists an element \(f \in A_1\), invertible in A. Then for every \(n \in \mathbb{Z}\), \(f^n\) is an element of \(A_n\) invertible in A, hence defines an isomorphism \(h \mapsto f^n h\) from \(A_0\) onto \(A_n\). Moreover, this shows that one obtains an isomorphism \(A_0[t,t^{-1}] \to A\) of graded \(A_0\)-algebras by sending t to f, which completes the proof of 4.7.

Then, under the conditions of 4.6, 4.7 already implies that the \(\mathscr{A}_m\) (\(m \in M\)) are invertible. To prove condition 4.1 b), we may therefore assume that \(\mathscr{A}_m\) and \(\mathscr{A}_{m'}\) have bases \(f_m\) and \(f_{m'}\), having inverses \(f_m^{-1} \in \Gamma(\mathscr{A}_{-m})\) and \(f_{m'}^{-1} \in \Gamma(\mathscr{A}_{-m'})\). Then multiplication by \(f_m^{-1} f_{m'}^{-1} \in \Gamma(\mathscr{A}_{-m-m'})\) defines a homomorphism \(\mathscr{A}_{m+m'} \to \mathscr{A}_0 \simeq \mathscr{O}_S\), transforming the image of \(f_m \otimes f_{m'}\) into the section 1 of \(\mathscr{O}_S\). In the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{A}_m \otimes \mathscr{A}_{m'} \ar[rr]^-{u} \ar[drr]_-{w} & & \mathscr{A}_{m+m'} \ar[d]^-{v} \\ & & \mathscr{A}_0 \simeq \mathscr{O}_S } \] w and v are therefore epimorphisms of invertible sheaves, hence isomorphisms, hence u is an isomorphism. Q.E.D.
