pages.title: 5. Quotient of an affine scheme by a diagonalizable group acting freely

blocks.html: 5. Quotient of an affine scheme by a diagonalizable group acting freely
17 We denote by \(\mathscr{M}\) the set of faithfully flat quasi-compact morphisms, and we recall that torsors are considered in the sense of the (fpqc) topology.

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

Let S be a prescheme, M an ordinary commutative group, \(G = \mathrm{D}_S(M)\) the diagonalizable group over S that it defines, and P an S-prescheme affine over S on which G acts freely on the right.

Then the equivalence relation defined by G in P is \(\mathscr{M}\)-effective (cf. IV, 3.4), i.e. the quotient \(X = P/G\) exists and P is a torsor over X with group \(G_X = \mathrm{D}_X(M)\). Moreover, \(P/G\) is affine over S; more precisely, if P is defined by the algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) graded of type M, then \(P/G\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{A}_0)\), where \(\mathscr{A}_0 = \mathscr{A}^G\) is the component of degree 0 of \(\mathscr{A}\).

Proof. Set \(X = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{A}_0)\); then we have a natural morphism \(P \to X\), deduced from \(\mathscr{A}_0 \to \mathscr{A}\), which is invariant under the operations of G. In this way, P becomes an X-prescheme, affine over X, with group of operators \(G_X = \mathrm{D}_X(M)\), and the hypothesis that G acts freely on P/S implies that \(G_X\) acts freely on P/X. Everything comes down to showing that P is a principal homogeneous bundle under \(G_X\), using the fact that \(\mathscr{B}_0 = \mathscr{O}_X\), where \(\mathscr{B}\) is the algebra graded of type M over X that defines P/X. We may then assume \(X = S\) and S affine, so P is affine, given by a ring A graded of type M whose homogeneous part of degree m will be denoted \(A_m\), so that \(S = \mathrm{Spec}(A_0)\). Taking 4.6 into account, it remains to check that we have:
(x) \(A_m \cdot A_{-m} = A_0 \qquad\) for every \(m \in M\).

One observes moreover, by an immediate computation, that (x) is equivalent to saying that \(P \times_S G \to P \times_S P\) is a closed immersion, and not merely a monomorphism (according to the hypothesis that G acts freely), i.e. that the homomorphism on the affine rings \[ \theta : A \otimes_{A_0} A \longrightarrow A(M) \] is surjective.18 This yields 5.1 when one assumes that the equivalence relation defined by G in P is closed. We shall show, however, that this hypothesis is already a consequence of the fact that G acts freely (which is moreover implicitly contained in theorem 5.1, since \(G \times_S P\) must be isomorphic to \(P \times_X P\), which is closed in \(P \times_S P\) since X is affine over S and hence separated over S).

Let \(R = P \times_S G\). The hypothesis that G acts freely, i.e. that \(R \to P \times_S P\) is a monomorphism, can be written by saying that the diagonal morphism \[ R \longrightarrow R \underset{(P\times_S P)}{\times} R = R' \] is an isomorphism. We have \(R = \mathrm{Spec}(A(M))\) and
\(R' = \mathrm{Spec}(A(M\times M)/K)\)19

where K is the ideal generated by the elements of the form \[ x_m(e_{m,0} - e_{0,m}), \qquad \text{with } m \in M,\ x_m \in A_m; \] let \(\phi : A(M\times M) \to A(M)\) be the surjective ring homomorphism defined by \[ x\, e_{m,n} \longmapsto x\, e_{m+n} \qquad (m,n \in M,\ x \in A) \] (where the \(e_m\), resp. \(e_{m,n} = e_m \otimes e_n\), are the elements of the canonical basis of \(A(M)\), resp. \(A(M\times M)\)). Then the diagonal morphism \(R \to R'\) corresponds to the homomorphism \(\overline{\phi} : A(M\times M)/K \to A(M)\) obtained by passing to the quotient by K. Now the kernel of \(\overline{\phi}\) is the ideal \(K'\) generated by the \(d_m = e_{m,0} - e_{0,m}\).

We have \(K \subseteq K'\), and the hypothesis that G acts freely on P, i.e. that \(\overline{\phi}\) is an isomorphism, is equivalent to the equality \(K' = K\), which is expressed by the relations \[ (\mathrm{xx}) \qquad d_m \in K = \sum_p A(M\times M)\, A_p\, d_p, \qquad \text{for every } m \in M. \] Using the natural tri-grading of \(A(M\times M)\), and the fact that the first degree of \(d_m\) is zero, this means that one can write \(d_m\) as a sum of elements of the form \[ f\, e_{r,s}(e_{p,0} - e_{0,p}) \qquad \text{with } f \in A_{-p}\cdot A_p, \] and using the fact that the total degree of \(d_m\) is m, one may restrict to terms such that \(r+s+p = m\).
Hence we must have, for every \(m \in M\), an expression:

(xxx) \[ \left\{ \begin{aligned} d_m &= e_{m,0} - e_{0,m} = \sum_{r,s} \lambda_{r,s}(e_{m-s,s} - e_{r,m-r}) \\ &\text{with } \lambda_{r,s} \in J_p = A_p \cdot A_{-p} \subseteq A_0,\ p = m-(r+s). \end{aligned} \right. \]
From this we must deduce relation (x), i.e. the relations
(xxxx) \(J_n = A_0 \qquad\) for every \(n \in M.\)

Now for this, it suffices to establish the same relation modulo every maximal ideal of \(A_0\). Since the hypotheses made are invariant under such a reduction, we may already assume that \(A_0\) is a field.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2

Under the preceding conditions (with \(A_0\) a field), if \(M \neq 0\), there exists a \(p \in M - \{0\}\) such that \(J_p = A_0\).
Indeed, if this were not the case, then the sum in the last member of (xxx) would be zero, for every \(m \in M\), which is absurd.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3

Under the preceding conditions, but without assuming any longer that \(A_0\) is a field, there exist finitely many elements \(p_i \in M-\{0\}\) such that \(\sum_i J_{p_i} = A_0\).
Indeed, one applies the result 5.2 to the situations deduced from \(A/A_0\) by reduction modulo the maximal ideals of \(A_0\).20

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

Suppose again that \(A_0\) is a field. Then for every subgroup N of M such that \(N \neq M\), there exists a \(p \in M-N\) such that \(J_p = A_0\).

Indeed, let \(M' = M/N\), and consider the ring \(A'\) graded of type \(M'\), whose underlying ring is A, and whose grading is given by \[ A'_{m'} = \coprod_{m \in h^{-1}(m')} A_m, \] where \(h : M \to M' = M/N\) is the canonical homomorphism. Geometrically, this construction amounts to considering the subgroup \(G' = \mathrm{D}_S(M')\) of G, and the structure on P of a scheme with group of operators \(G'\) induced by the operations of G. It is then obvious that \(G'\) acts freely on \(P'\), i.e. the pair \((M', A')\) satisfies the hypotheses of 5.3. We therefore obtain \[ 1 = \sum_i f_i g_i \qquad \text{with } f_i \in A'_{m'_i}\ ,\ g_i \in A'_{-m'_i}\ \text{ and }\ m'_i \in M'-\{0\}, \] whence immediately the conclusion 5.4 by taking the components of the right-hand side along \(A_0\), and using the fact that \(A_0\) is a field.

Note now that \[ J_p \cdot J_q \subseteq J_{p+q} \qquad \text{and} \qquad J_p = J_{-p}, \] so if N denotes the set of \(m \in M\) such that \(J_m = A_0\), we see that N is a subgroup of M. Using 5.4 we see that it is equal to M. This completes the proof of theorem 5.1.
As we pointed out in the course of the proof, theorem 5.1 implies:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Under the conditions of 5.1, the graph morphism \[ P \underset{S}{\times} G \longrightarrow P \underset{S}{\times} P \] is a closed immersion.
One concludes immediately:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6

Let \(\sigma\) be a section of P over S. Then the morphism \(g \mapsto \sigma \cdot g\) from G to P defined by \(\sigma\) is a closed immersion.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.7

Let G, H be two S-groups, with G diagonalizable, H affine over S, and let \(u : G \to H\) be a homomorphism of S-groups which is a monomorphism. Then u is a closed immersion, \(H/G = X\) exists and H is a principal homogeneous bundle over X with group \(G_X\); finally, X is affine over S.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.8

Under the conditions of 5.1, if P is of finite type (resp. of finite presentation) over S, the same holds for \(X = P/G\).

Indeed, it follows from the hypothesis that the fibers of \(G_X\) are of finite type, so \(G_X\) is of finite presentation over X by 2.1 b); hence P, being a torsor under \(G_X\), is of finite presentation over X21. Since it is also faithfully flat over X, our conclusion then follows from Exp. V, Prop. 9.1.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: The following sentence has been added.
N.D.E.: Indeed, for every \(b \in A\), \(a_m \in A_m\), we have \(\theta(b\otimes a_m) = ba_m\otimes e_m\), so the surjectivity of \(\theta\) is equivalent to (x).

N.D.E.: We have denoted by K the ideal denoted J in the original, in order to distinguish it from the ideals \(J_p\) of \(A_0\) that appear in (xxx). On the other hand, it has been made explicit further on that the relations (xx) are equivalent to the equality \(K = K'\) (see what follows).
N.D.E.: It follows from 5.2 that \(\sum_{p\neq 0} J_p = A_0\), so 1 can be written as a finite sum \(\sum_i x_i\), with \(x_i \in J_{p_i}\).
N.D.E.: cf. EGA II, 2.7.1 (vi).

pages.title: 6. Essentially free morphisms, and representability of certain functors of the form \(\prod_{\mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{Y}\)

blocks.html: 6. Essentially free morphisms, and representability of certain functors of the form \(\prod_{\mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{Y}\)
*

blocks.title: Définition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of preschemes. We say that \(f\) is essentially free, or that X is essentially free over S, if one can find a covering of S by affine open sets \(S_i\), for each i an \(S_i\)-prescheme \(S'_i\) affine and faithfully flat over \(S_i\), and a covering \((X'_{ij})_j\) of \(X'_i = X \times_S S'_i\) by affine open sets \(X'_{ij}\), such that for every \((i,j)\), the ring of \(X'_{ij}\) is a free module over the ring of \(S'_i\).22

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.2

a) If X is essentially free over S, it is flat over S, the converse being true if S is artinian.

b) If S is the spectrum of a field, every S-prescheme is essentially free over S.

c) If X is essentially free over S, and if \(S' \to S\) is a base change morphism, then \(X' = X \times_S S'\) is essentially free over \(S'\). The converse is true if \(S' \to S\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact.
The proof is immediate, using for the converse in a) the fact that a flat module over an artinian local ring is free.23

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.3

Let H be a diagonalizable S-group prescheme (more generally, one that becomes diagonalizable by a suitable faithfully flat quasi-compact extension of every affine open subset of S, i.e. H is "of multiplicative type", cf. IX 1.1). Then H is essentially free over S.
Indeed, if H is diagonalizable, it is affine over S and defined by an algebra which is a free \(\mathscr{O}_S\)-module.
The introduction of definition 6.1 is justified by the following

blocks.title: Théorème
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.4

Let S be a prescheme, Z an essentially free S-prescheme, Y a closed subprescheme of Z. Consider the following functor24 \[ \mathrm{F} = \prod_{\mathrm{Z}/\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{Y}/\mathrm{Z} : (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S} \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Ens}), \qquad \mathrm{F}(S') = \Gamma(\mathrm{Y}_{S'}/\mathrm{Z}_{S'}) = \begin{cases} \varnothing & \text{if } \mathrm{Z}_{S'} \neq \mathrm{Y}_{S'}\,; \\ \{\mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{Z}_{S'}}\} & \text{if } \mathrm{Z}_{S'} = \mathrm{Y}_{S'}. \end{cases} \] This functor is representable by a closed subprescheme of S.25

26 Note first that F is a sheaf for the (fpqc) topology: since \(\mathrm{F}(S') = \varnothing\) or \(\{\mathrm{pt}\}\) for every \(S'\), this reduces to checking that if \((S_i)\) is an open covering of S (resp. \(S' \to S\) a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism), and if each \(Y_{S_i} \to Z_{S_i}\) (resp. if \(Y_{S'} \to Z_{S'}\)) is an isomorphism, then the same holds for \(Y \to Z\); now this is clear (resp. follows from SGA 1, VIII 5.4 or EGA IV2, 2.7.1).

Moreover, by SGA 1, VIII 2.1 and 5.5, faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphisms are of effective descent for the fibered category of closed immersion arrows. This allows us to restrict ourselves, with the notation of 6.1, to the case where \(S = S'_i\).

Let then \((Z_j)\) be a covering of Z by affine open sets such that \(\mathscr{O}(Z_j)\) is a free module over \(A = \mathscr{O}(S)\), and let \(Y_j = Y \cap Z_j\) and \(F_j : (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}_{/S} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) be the functor defined in terms of \((Z_j, Y_j)\) as F is in terms of \((Z,Y)\). It is a subfunctor of the final functor, and we obviously have \(F = \bigcap_j F_j\), which reduces us to proving that each \(F_j\) is representable by a closed subscheme \(T_j\) of S (for then F will be representable by the closed subscheme T which is the intersection of the \(T_j\)). We may therefore assume Z affine as well, \(Z = \mathrm{Spec}(B)\), where B is a free A-module. Let J be a subset of B defining the subscheme Y of Z, and let K be the ideal in A generated by the \(u_i(J) \subseteq A\), where the \(u_i : B \to A\) are the coordinate forms with respect to the chosen basis. One sees immediately that \(T = V(K) = \mathrm{Spec}(A/K)\) satisfies the required condition, which completes the proof.

Examples 6.5. — Let us give some important examples of functors which reduce to functors \(\prod_{Z/S} Y/Z\) of the type envisaged in 6.4 and for which it is useful in the sequel to have representability criteria. We denote by S a prescheme, and by X, Y, Z etc. preschemes over S.
a) Suppose given an S-morphism
(x) \(q : X \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,Z),\)
("X acts on Y, with values in Z"), i.e. a morphism
(xx) \(r : X \underset{S}{\times} Y \longrightarrow Z.\)

Consider a subprescheme \(Z'\) of Z, whence a monomorphism \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,Z') \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,Z) \] which makes the first functor a subfunctor of the second; let \(X'\) be the inverse image of this subfunctor under (x); it is the subfunctor of X such that \(X'(T)\) is the set of \(x \in X(T)\) such that \(q(x) : Y_T \to Z_T\) factors through \(Z'_T\). This functor \(X'\) can be described as follows: set \(P = X \times_S Y\), let \(P'\) be the inverse image of \(Z'\) under \(r : P \to Z\); then we have an obvious isomorphism
(xxx) \(X' \simeq \prod_{P/X} P'/P.\)
One therefore obtains: if Y is essentially free over S and \(Z'\) is closed in Z, the subfunctor \(X'\) of X is representable by a closed subprescheme of X.

b) Suppose given two ways of making X act on Y with values in Z, i.e. two morphisms \[ q_1, q_2 : X \rightrightarrows \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,Z), \] and set \(X' = \mathrm{Ker}(q_1,q_2)\): this is the subfunctor of X such that \(X'(T)\) is the set of \(x \in X(T)\) such that the two morphisms \(q_1(x), q_2(x) : Y_T \rightrightarrows Z_T\) are equal. Now giving \(q_1, q_2\) is equivalent to giving a morphism \[ q : X \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y, Z\underset{S}{\times}Z), \] or again, a morphism \(r : X \times_S Y \to Z \times_S Z\); then set \(U = Z \times_S Z\), let \(U'\) be the diagonal subprescheme of \(Z \times_S Z\); then \(X'\) is none other than the inverse image of the subfunctor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,U') \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,U)\) under q, so it can be put in the form (xxx), with \(P = X \times_S Y\), and \(P'\) = the inverse image of the diagonal under r, i.e. the kernel of \(X \times_S Y \underset{r_2}{\overset{r_1}{\rightrightarrows}} Z\). We are therefore under the conditions of (a).
Consequently one sees that: if Y is essentially free over S and Z is separated over S, then the subfunctor \(X'\) of X is representable by a closed subprescheme of X.

c) Suppose given a morphism \(q : X \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,Y)\), i.e. "X acts on Y". Let \(X'\) be the "kernel" of this morphism, i.e. the subfunctor \(X'\) of X such that \(X'(T)\) is the set of \(x \in X(T)\) such that \(q(x) : Y_T \to Y_T\) is the identity. This functor comes under b), as one sees by introducing a second homomorphism \[ q' : X \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(Y,Y) \] "by making X act trivially on Y". Hence: if Y is essentially free over S and separated over S, the kernel subfunctor of q is representable by a closed subprescheme of X.

d) Under the conditions of c), consider the subfunctor \(Y'\) of Y "of invariants under X", so \(Y'(T)\) is the set of \(y \in Y(T)\) such that the corresponding morphism \(\overline{q}(y) : X_T \to Y_T\) is "the constant T-morphism with value y". Introducing \(q'\) as in c), and the homomorphisms corresponding to q and \(q'\): \[ \overline{q}, \overline{q'} : Y \rightrightarrows \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_S(X,Y), \] one sees that \(Y'\) is precisely \(\mathrm{Ker}(\overline{q},\overline{q'})\), and therefore again comes under b) (with the roles of X, Y reversed and \(Z = Y\)).
Consequently, if X is essentially free over S and Y separated over S, then the subfunctor \(Y'\) of Y of invariants under X is representable by a closed subprescheme of Y.

e) Constructions of the type made explicit in the preceding examples are especially frequent in group theory. Thus, when G is an S-group prescheme acting on the S-prescheme X: \[ q : G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_S(X), \] the kernel of q ("the subgroup of G acting trivially") is a closed subscheme of G provided that X is essentially free and separated over S (example c)), and the subobject \(X^G\) of invariants is a closed subprescheme of X, provided that G is essentially free over S, and X separated over S27 (example d)).

Let Y, Z be subpreschemes of X; consider the subfunctor \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(Y,Z)\) of G ("transporter of Y into Z") whose points with values in a T over S are the \(g \in G(T)\) such that the corresponding automorphism of \(X_T\) satisfies \(g(Y_T) \subseteq Z_T\), i.e. induces a morphism \(Y_T \to X_T\) factoring through \(Y_T \to Z_T\). Hence: if Y is essentially free over S, and Z closed in X, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(Y,Z)\) is a closed subprescheme of G (example a)).

One may also consider the strict transporter of Y into Z,28 whose points with values in a T over S are the \(g \in G(T)\) such that \(g(Y_T) = Z_T\), which is none other than \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(Y,Z) \cap \sigma(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(Z,Y))\), where \(\sigma\) is the symmetry of G. Consequently, if Y and Z are essentially free over S and closed in X, the strict transporter of Y into Z is a closed subprescheme of G.

An important case is that where \(X = G\), with G acting on itself by inner automorphisms. If H is a subprescheme of G, the strict transporter of H into H is also called the normalizer of H in G, and denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G H\). Hence: if H is a closed subgroup prescheme of G, essentially free over S, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G H\) is representable by a closed subgroup prescheme of G.

Finally, let Z be a subprescheme of G; then its centralizer \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(Z)\) in G is the subgroup functor of G defined by the procedure of d), when one considers that "Z acts on G" by the operations induced by those of G; hence if Z is essentially free over S and G is separated over S, \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(Z)\) is a closed subgroup prescheme of G. In particular, if G is essentially free and separated over S, then the center C of G, which is none other than \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(G)\), is a closed subgroup prescheme of G.

When S is the spectrum of a field, 6.3 b) shows that in examples a) through e) above, the "essentially free" conditions are automatically satisfied; only separation conditions remain. Recalling that a group prescheme over a field is necessarily separated, one finds for example:

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.7

29 Let G be a group prescheme over a field k. Then:

– For every subprescheme Z of G, the centralizer of Z in G is a closed subgroup prescheme of G; this is in particular the case for the center \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_G(G)\) of G.

– More generally, if \(u,v : X \to G\) are morphisms of preschemes, \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(u,v)\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of G.

– For two subpreschemes Y, Z of G, with Z closed, \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_G(Y,Z)\) is a closed subprescheme of G. If Y is also closed, the same conclusion holds for \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(Y,Z)\).

– For every subgroup prescheme30 H of G, the normalizer \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_G(H)\) is a closed subgroup scheme of G.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 6.8

31 Let A be a commutative ring, M an A-module, \(M^{\vee} = \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,A)\); equip the A-module \(\mathrm{End}_A(M)\) with the topology of discrete pointwise convergence, that is, a base of neighborhoods of 0 is formed by the following sub-A-modules, where \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(m_1,\ldots,m_n \in M\): \[ K(m_1,\ldots,m_n) = \{u \in \mathrm{End}_A(M) \mid u(m_i) = 0 \text{ for } i=1,\ldots,n\}. \] We say that M is a quasi-free A-module if the image of the canonical morphism \(\Theta : M\otimes_A M^{\vee} \to \mathrm{End}_A(M)\) contains \(\mathrm{id}_M\) in its closure, that is, if the following condition is satisfied:

(*) for all \(m_1,\ldots,m_n \in M\), there exist \(x_1,\ldots,x_r \in M\) and \(f_1,\ldots,f_r \in M^{\vee}\) such that \(m_i = \Theta\bigl(\sum_{s=1}^r x_s \otimes f_s\bigr)(m_i) = \sum_{s=1}^r f_s(m_i)\, x_s\) for \(i = 1,\ldots,n\).

(In this case, \(\mathrm{Im}\,\Theta\) is dense in \(\mathrm{End}_A(M)\) since for every \(u \in \mathrm{End}_A(M)\), we have \(u(m_i) = \sum_{s=1}^r f_s(m_i)\, u(x_s) = \Theta\bigl(\sum_{s=1}^r u(x_s)\otimes f_s\bigr)(m_i)\).)

Note first that this property is stable under base change. Indeed, let \(\varphi : A \to A'\) be a ring morphism, \(M' = M \otimes_A A'\), and \(m'_1,\ldots,m'_n \in M'\); then \(m'_i = \sum_j m_{ij}\otimes b_{ij}\) (\(m_{ij}\in M,\ b_{ij}\in A'\)); by hypothesis, there exist \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\in M\) and \(f_1,\ldots,f_r \in \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,A)\) such that \(m_{ij} = \sum_s x_s f_s(m_{ij})\) for all \(i,j\). Denote by \(\varphi\circ f_s\) the image of \(f_s\) in \(\mathrm{Hom}_A(M,A') = \mathrm{Hom}_{A'}(M',A')\); then for every \(i=1,\ldots,n\) we have: \[ \Bigl(\sum_s x_s\otimes \varphi\circ f_s\Bigr)(m'_i) = \sum_{s,j} x_s\otimes\varphi(f_s(m_{ij}))\,b_{ij} = \sum_j \Bigl(\sum_s x_s f_s(m_{ij})\Bigr)\otimes b_{ij} = m'_i, \] which proves that \(M'\) is quasi-free over \(A'\).

Note also that every projective A-module P is quasi-free (there exist A-morphisms \(A^{(\mathrm{I})} \xrightarrow{\ \pi\ } P \xrightarrow{\ \tau\ } A^{(\mathrm{I})}\) such that \(\pi\circ\tau = \mathrm{id}_P\); denote by \((e_i)\) the canonical basis of \(A^{(\mathrm{I})}\) and by \(f_i\) the linear form \(e_i^*\circ\tau\) on P; if \(m_1,\ldots,m_n\in P\), there exists a finite subset J of I such that \(m_k = \sum_{i\in J} f_i(m_k)\,\pi(e_i)\) for \(k=1,\ldots,n\)).

Then, theorem 6.4 remains valid if in the statement of definition 6.1 the word "free" is replaced by "projective" or, more generally, by "quasi-free". Indeed, proceeding as in the proof of 6.4, we are reduced to proving the following

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.8.1

Let M be a quasi-free A-module, N a submodule, F the covariant functor \((\text{A-algebras}) \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) such that \(\mathrm{F}(B) = \{\mathrm{pt}\}\) if \(M_B = (M/N)_B\), and \(\mathrm{F}(B) = \varnothing\) otherwise. Then there exists an ideal K of A such that \(\mathrm{F}(B) = \{\mathrm{pt}\}\) if and only if the morphism \(A \to B\) factors through A/K, i.e. we have an isomorphism functorial in B: \[ \mathrm{F}(B) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\text{A-alg.}}(A/K, B). \]

Proof. Let \((n_j)\) be a system of generators of N, and let \(F_j\) be the subfunctor of the final functor \(\underline{e}\) (\(\underline{e}(B) = \{\mathrm{pt}\}\) for every B) corresponding to the submodule \(An_j\). We have \(F(B) = \{\mathrm{pt}\}\) if and only if the image of each \(n_j\) in \(M_B\) is zero, so F is the intersection of the functors \(F_j\). This reduces us to the case where N is generated by a single element n.

Let \(\varphi : A \to B\) be an A-algebra; if the image \(n\otimes 1\) of n in \(M_B\) is zero, then for every \(f \in M^{\vee} = \mathrm{Hom}_A(M,A)\) we have \(0 = f(n)\otimes 1 = \varphi(f(n))\). On the other hand, since M is quasi-free, there exist \(x_1,\ldots,x_r\in M\) and \(f_1,\ldots,f_r\in M^{\vee}\) such that \(n = \sum_s f_s(n)\,x_s\), whence \(n\otimes 1 = \sum_s x_s\otimes\varphi(f_s(n))\). It follows that \(n\otimes 1 = 0\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through A/K, where K is the ideal generated by the \(f_s(n)\) for \(s=1,\ldots,r\). This proves the lemma.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: One may replace "free" by "projective", cf. 6.8 below. On the other hand, this notion should be compared with that of a flat and pure S-prescheme, introduced and developed in [RG71]; see in particular loc. cit., part 1, 3.3.12 and part 2, 3.1.4.1.

N.D.E.: Indeed, let \((A,\mathfrak{m})\) be an artinian local ring, k its residue field, M an arbitrary A-module, \((x_i)_{i\in I}\) elements of M whose images form a basis of M/\(\mathfrak{m}\)M over k. Let F be the free A-module with basis \((e_i)_{i\in I}\), and \(\varphi : F \to M\) the A-morphism defined by \(\varphi(e_i) = x_i\). Then \(Q = \mathrm{Coker}\,\varphi\) satisfies \(Q = \mathfrak{m}Q\), whence, since \(\mathfrak{m}\) is nilpotent, \(Q=0\). Suppose moreover M flat over A; then \(K = \mathrm{Ker}\,\varphi\) satisfies \(K\otimes_A k = 0\), i.e. \(K = \mathfrak{m}K\), whence \(K=0\).
N.D.E.: cf. II.1, where this functor is denoted \(\prod_{Z/S} Y\).
N.D.E.: Z has been corrected to S.
N.D.E.: The original has been expanded in what follows; see also 1.7.3.
N.D.E.: \(S_X\) has been corrected to S.
N.D.E.: denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Transpstr}}_G(Y,Z)\).
N.D.E.: The numbering of the original has been retained: there is no 6.6.
N.D.E.: Indeed, over a field k, every subgroup prescheme of G is closed, cf. VIA, 0.6.1.
N.D.E.: The original has been expanded in what follows; in particular lemma 6.8.1 has been added.
The present section is independent of the theory of diagonalizable groups; its natural place would be in VIB.

pages.title: 7. Appendix: On monomorphisms of group preschemes

blocks.html: 7. Appendix: On monomorphisms of group preschemes

The result proved in the present section is not needed for the rest of the seminar, except for X 8.8 and XV and XVI. It relies in an essential way on the existence theorem for quotient groups of Exposé VIA.32
Corollary 5.7 leads one to ask under what conditions one can assert that a monomorphism \(u : G \to H\) of S-groups is an immersion, or even a closed immersion.

We saw in VIB 1.4.2 that this is the case if S is the spectrum of a field, provided that G is of finite type over k and H locally of finite type over k. One easily concludes that the same result remains valid if one only assumes S artinian.33

On the other hand, it is easy to give examples of bijective monomorphisms which are not immersions, S being for example the affine line over a field, or the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring. One takes for example \(H = (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_S\), \(G = G_1 \times_S G_2\), where \(G_1\) is the open subgroup of H, the complement of the closed point x distinct from 0 of the fiber \(H_s \simeq \mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\) (where s denotes a fixed closed point of S), and \(G_2\) the closed subscheme of H which is the sum of the subscheme reduced to the unit section and the reduced closed subscheme defined by the closed point x. One easily checks that \(G_2\) is indeed stable under the multiplication of H, and hence is a group scheme. The immersions \(G_i \to H\) (\(i=1,2\)) then define a homomorphism of S-groups \(G = G_1 \times G_2 \to H\), which is obviously a bijective monomorphism (and in addition a local immersion), but is not an immersion. (One checks that G and H are reduced, G having three disjoint irreducible components, whereas H has only two irreducible components).

Note that \(G_1 \to H\) also gives an example of an open subgroup G of H which is not closed (contrary to what happens for algebraic groups over a field). The theory of degeneration of elliptic curves provides other examples of this last phenomenon, with moreover H smooth over S of relative dimension 1, and G with connected fibers.

It is possible,** on the other hand, that as soon as one assumes G flat over S, and (say) G and H of finite presentation over S, a monomorphism \(u : G \to H\) of S-groups is automatically an immersion. We are going to prove a result of this nature, under additional hypotheses.

Note first that we may assume S affine, and (thanks to the finite presentation hypothesis on G and H, which allows us to reduce to the case of the spectrum of a ring of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\)34) S noetherian. Then G and H are noetherian. To say that \(u : G \to H\) is a closed immersion (resp. an immersion) then amounts to saying that u is a monomorphism (which is true by hypothesis) and that u is proper (resp. and that u is proper at every point of \(u(G)\))35. The valuative criterion of properness assures us that it suffices to check that for every base change \(S' \to S\), with \(S'\) the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, complete if one wishes, the morphism \(u' : G' \to H'\) has the same properness property. (The case of local properness was forgotten in EGA II 7.3, and will appear as an erratum in EGA IV*). This therefore reduces us to the case where S is itself the spectrum of a complete discrete valuation ring, — provided that the additional hypotheses on G, H, u that we shall be led to formulate are stable under base change.

Let then s (resp. \(s'\)) be the closed point (resp. the generic point) of S. Then the homomorphisms induced on the fibers \[ u_s : G_s \longrightarrow H_s \qquad \text{and} \qquad u_{s'} : G_{s'} \longrightarrow H_{s'} \] are closed immersions, since they are monomorphisms of group schemes of finite type over fields (VIB 1.4.2). We may therefore identify \(G_{s'}\) with a closed subscheme of \(H_{s'}\). Now we have the following result:

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1

Let S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, \(s'\) its generic point, H an S-prescheme, \(L'\) a closed subprescheme of the generic fiber \(H_{s'}\), so that \(L'\) is also a subprescheme of H.

Then the schematic closure L in H (i.e. the smallest closed subprescheme of H dominating \(L'\), cf. EGA I 9.5) exists and is also the unique closed subprescheme of H, flat over S, whose generic fiber is \(L'\). Moreover, the formation of L is functorial with respect to a variable pair \((H,L')\), and commutes with the formation of cartesian products over S.

In particular, if H is an S-group and \(L'\) a subgroup of \(H_{s'}\), then L is a subgroup of H.

The proof is immediate and left to the reader.** Applying this to the situation H, \(G_{s'}\), one sees that the monomorphism \(u : G \to H\) factors as \(G \to L \to H\), where L is a subgroup of H which is a closed subprescheme, flat over S, and where \(G \to L\) induces an isomorphism on the generic fibers. Then \(u : G \to H\) is an immersion (resp. a closed immersion) if and only if \(u' : G \to L\) is. This therefore reduces us to the case where H is flat over S and \(u_{s'} : G_{s'} \to H_{s'}\) an isomorphism (provided that the additional hypotheses that we may have to formulate on G, H, u are respected when H is replaced by a closed subgroup prescheme). Since H is then the schematic closure of \(H_{s'}\), if u is an immersion, then \(u(G)\) will be a subprescheme36 of H dominating \(H_{s'}\), so its schematic closure will also be H, and consequently \(u(G)\) will be an open subprescheme of H. Consequently, we shall in fact have to prove that u is an open immersion (resp. an isomorphism if we wanted to establish that u is a closed immersion). Since G and H are flat over S, it amounts to the same to say that the morphisms induced on the fibers are open immersions, resp. isomorphisms (cf. SGA 1, I 5.7), and since this is already the case for \(u_{s'}\), we are reduced to proving that \(u_s\) is an open immersion, resp. an isomorphism.

Note first that, since G and H are flat over S, the dimension of their fibers remains constant (VIB 4.3). Since G and H have the same generic fiber, it follows that this dimension is the same for G and H, so \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is a monomorphism of algebraic groups of the same dimension. One easily concludes that \(G_s\) is open in \(H_s\), and in fact is set-theoretically a union of connected components of \(H_s\) (one is reduced to the case where the base field is algebraically closed, and G and H reduced, hence smooth over k, where this is immediate…). Hence \(H_s - G_s\) is closed in \(H_s\), hence in H, so its complement \(H'\) in H is open, and it is obviously an open set stable under the group law of H. Hence, replacing H by \(H'\) if necessary, we may, in order to prove that u is an immersion, reduce (with the usual proviso) to the case where, in addition to the preceding hypotheses, u is assumed bijective, i.e. \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) bijective. In any case we are thus reduced to proving that u, or again \(u_s\), is an isomorphism, under the bijectivity hypothesis where applicable.

So suppose first that u is bijective. If \(H_s\) is reduced, we can obviously conclude that \(u_s\) is an isomorphism, for \(G_s\) is identified with a closed subscheme of \(H_s\) having the same underlying set. In particular, if \(k = \kappa(s)\) is of characteristic zero, every algebraic group over k is reduced by Cartier (cf. VIB, 1.6.1, or VIIB, 3.3.1, or EGA IV4, 16.12.2 and 17.12.5) and we have thus obtained:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.2

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a homomorphism of group preschemes of finite presentation over S. Suppose that u is a monomorphism, G flat over S, and the residue fields of S of characteristic zero. Then u is an immersion.

When \(\kappa(s)\) is of characteristic \(p>0\), we shall restrict ourselves to the case where G is commutative. Then (with the reductions made) H is also commutative, since it is the schematic closure of \(H_{s'}\), which is isomorphic to \(G_{s'}\), hence commutative. For every integer \(n \geqslant 0\), we set \(S_n = \mathrm{Spec}(V/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1})\) (where V is the valuation ring that defines S and \(\mathfrak{m}\) its maximal ideal), \(G_n = G \times_S S_n\), \(H_n = H \times_S S_n\). For every integer \(m>0\), we also introduce the subgroups \({}_mG\) and \({}_mH\) of G and H, the kernels of the m-th power. We likewise define \({}_m(G_n) = ({}_mG)_n\), which will simply be written \({}_mG_n\), and similarly for H.

By virtue of VIA 3.2, one can form the quotients \(Q_n = H_n/G_n\); then \(Q_n\) is a commutative group scheme over \(S_n\), flat over \(S_n\), and \(H_n \to Q_n\) is a faithfully flat morphism with kernel \(G_n\). Since the formation of quotients commutes with base extension37, we have \[ Q_n \simeq Q_m \underset{S_m}{\times} S_n \qquad \text{for } m \geqslant n, \] in particular the fiber \(Q_n \times_{S_n} S_0\) is none other than \(Q_0 = H_0/G_0\). Since \(G_0\) has the same underlying set as \(H_0\), \(Q_0\) is set-theoretically reduced to a single point: it is a purely infinitesimal group. Consequently, each \(Q_n\) is finite and flat over \(S_n\). Hence \(Q_n\) is defined by an algebra \(C_n\) over \(V_n = V/\mathfrak{m}^{n+1}\) which is a free module of finite type over this ring, and for \(m \geqslant n\), we have \(C_n = C_m \otimes_{V_m} V_n\), an isomorphism respecting the diagonal map as well. One therefore obtains a free module of finite type \(C = \varprojlim C_n\) over \(V = \varprojlim V_n\), and the diagonal maps of the \(C_n\) define a diagonal map of C, so that \(Q = \mathrm{Spec}(C)\) becomes a group scheme finite and flat over S, such that \[ Q \underset{S}{\times} S_n = Q_n \] for every n.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3

38 Let K be a field, Q a finite group scheme over K, of degree n. Then the n-th power morphism in Q is trivial.
Cf. VIIA 8.5.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 7.3.1

Statement 7.3 still makes sense for a group scheme Q/S finite and locally free over S, S being an arbitrary base prescheme. It would be interesting to find a proof in this general case.

Note that 7.3 (i.e. VIIA, 8.5) proves in any case that the statement in question is true if S is reduced, as one sees by applying 7.3 to the fibers of Q at the maximal points (i.e. generic points of the irreducible components) of S.39

In particular, under the conditions of the preceding proof, where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring and Q is commutative, one finds that \(n\cdot\mathrm{id}_Q = 0\). Moreover, here n is a power \(p^{\nu_0}\) of the residue characteristic, and one finds the following

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.4

Q and the \(Q_n\) being as above, and their common degree being \(p^{\nu_0}\), we shall have \(p^{\nu_0}\cdot\mathrm{id}_Q = 0\) and consequently \(p^{\nu_0}\cdot\mathrm{id}_{Q_n} = 0\) for every n.

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.5

Suppose moreover \(G_0\) smooth over k, and the homomorphism \(p\cdot\mathrm{id}_{G_0}\) flat (which amounts to saying, by virtue of the structure of algebraic groups over an algebraically closed field \(\overline{k}\), that \(G_{\overline{k}}\) contains no subgroup isomorphic to the additive group). Then for every \(\nu \geqslant \nu_0\) and \(n>0\), the group \({}_{p^\nu}H_n\) is flat over \(S_n\).

Indeed, it follows from \(p^{\nu_0}\cdot\mathrm{id}_{Q_n} = 0\) that \(p^\nu\cdot\mathrm{id}_{H_n}\) factors through \(G_n\), so that we shall have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ H_n \ar[rr]^-{p^{\nu}\cdot\mathrm{id}_{H_n}} & & H_n \\ G_n \ar[u]^-{u_n} \ar[rr]_-{p^{\nu}\cdot\mathrm{id}_{G_n}} \ar[urr]_(0.3){v_n} & & G_n \ar[u]_-{u_n} } \]

I claim that \(v_n\) is flat. Indeed, since \(H_n\) and \(G_n\) are flat over \(S_n\), we are reduced to checking that \(v_0\) is flat (SGA 1, IV 5.9), so we may assume that \(n=0\), whence \(S_n = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\). Since \(p\cdot\mathrm{id}_{G_0}\), and hence \(p^\nu\cdot\mathrm{id}_{G_0}\), is flat, its image is an induced open subgroup \(G'_0\) of \(G_0\), and since \(u_0 : G_0 \to H_0\) is surjective, it follows that \(v_0\) takes its values in \(G'_0\), so it may be considered as a homomorphism into \(G'_0\). Since its composite with \(u_0\) is an epimorphism, it is an epimorphism, hence it is a flat homomorphism into \(G'_0\), hence a flat homomorphism into \(G_0\). Therefore \(v_n\) is flat, hence \(\mathrm{Ker}\, v_n = {}_{p^\nu}H\) is flat over S. Q.E.D.

Remark. — We have not explicitly used the fact that \(G_0\) is smooth over k; but it is easy to see that it is a consequence of the fact that \(p\cdot\mathrm{id}_{G}\) is flat, which is why we made this condition explicit in the hypothesis of corollary 7.5.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.6

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a surjective monomorphism of commutative algebraic groups over a field k of characteristic \(p>0\), and consider the (purely infinitesimal) group \(Q = H/G\); then there exists an integer \(\nu_1\) such that for \(\nu \geqslant \nu_1\) the sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow {}_{p^\nu}G \longrightarrow {}_{p^\nu}H \longrightarrow Q \longrightarrow 0 \] is exact.
It suffices to ensure exactness at Q, and for this to ensure that the homomorphism
(x) \(\mathscr{O}_{Q,e} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{p^\nu H, e}\)
of the local rings at the neutral elements is injective (N.B. remember that Q is set-theoretically reduced to the element e). Now there is a natural homomorphism
(xx) \(\mathscr{O}_{p^\nu H, e} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{H,e}/\mathfrak{m}^{p^\nu},\)

where \(\mathfrak{m}\) is the augmentation ideal (i.e. the maximal ideal) of \(\mathscr{O}_{H,e}\), as one sees by noting that \(p^\nu\cdot\mathrm{id}_H\) vanishes on the kernel of the "iterated Frobenius homomorphism" \(\mathrm{F}^\nu\); the composite of the homomorphisms (x) and (xx) is also equal to the natural composite
(xxx) \(\mathscr{O}_{Q,e} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{H,e} \longrightarrow \mathscr{O}_{H,e}/\mathfrak{m}^{p^\nu}.\)

Now \(\mathscr{O}_{Q,e} \to \mathscr{O}_{H,e}\) is injective, since \(H \to Q\) is an epimorphism and hence flat; on the other hand \(\mathscr{O}_{Q,e}\) is artinian; finally, the intersection in \(\mathscr{O}_{H,e}\) of the \(\mathfrak{m}^{p^\nu}\) is reduced to 0, hence so is the intersection of their traces on \(\mathscr{O}_{Q,e}\). Consequently one of these traces is reduced to 0, which proves that (xxx) is injective, and a fortiori (x) is injective.

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.7

Under the conditions of 7.5, there exists a \(\nu_1\) such that, for \(\nu \geqslant \nu_1\) and every n, the sequence of \(S_n\)-groups \[ 0 \longrightarrow {}_{p^\nu}G_n \longrightarrow {}_{p^\nu}H_n \xrightarrow{\ w_n\ } Q_n \longrightarrow 0 \] is exact (more precisely, \(w_n\) is faithfully flat and its kernel is \({}_{p^\nu}G_n\)).

One takes \( u_1\) as in 7.6 applied to \(u_0 : G_0 o H_0\), with \( u_1 \geqslant u_0\) (where \(p^{ u_0} = \mathrm{rank}\, Q_0\)). It only remains to check that \(w_n\) is faithfully flat. Now, by virtue of 7.5, \({}_{p^ u}H\) is flat over \(S_n\), and since \(Q_n\) is too, we are reduced to checking that \(w_0 : {}_{p^ u}H_0 o Q_0\) is faithfully flat, i.e. is an epimorphism, which is true by the choice of \( u_1\).

blocks.title: Corollaire
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.8

Suppose moreover H separated over S, or more generally that \({}_{p^ u}H\) is separated over S for every \( u\), and that \({}_{p^ u}G\) is finite over S for every \( u\). Then the group schemes \({}_{p^ u}G\) and \({}_{p^ u}H\) are finite and flat over S for \( u \geqslant u_1\), and we have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow {}_{p^ u}G \longrightarrow {}_{p^ u}H \xrightarrow{\ w\ } Q \longrightarrow 0. \]

Since \(u : G o H\) is surjective, the same holds for the induced morphism \({}_{p^ u}G o {}_{p^ u}H\), and since the first member is finite over S and the second separated over S, it follows that the second member is finite over S. To check then that \({}_{p^ u}G\) and \({}_{p^ u}H\) are also flat over S, it suffices to check that this is the case for \({}_{p^ u}G_n\) and \({}_{p^ u}H_n\) for every n, which is contained in 7.5. Finally, the exact sequence of 7.8 comes from the exact sequences 7.7 for variable n.

Taking the generic fibers in the exact sequence 7.8 and remembering that \(u_{s'} : G_{s'} \to H_{s'}\) is an isomorphism, one finds \(Q_{s'}\) = the unit group, whence (since Q is flat over S) Q = the unit group, whence \(Q_s\) = the unit group, so \(u_s : G_s \to H_s\) is an isomorphism. Hence:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.9

Let \(u : G \to H\) be a homomorphism of group preschemes of finite presentation over the prescheme S. We assume:

a) u is a monomorphism.

b) G is flat over S.

c) For every \(s \in S\) such that \(\kappa(s)\) is of residue characteristic \(p>0\), we require that the following conditions be satisfied for the homomorphism \(u' : G' \to H'\) of group preschemes over \(S' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{S,s})\) deduced from \(u : G \to H\) by the base change \(S' \to S\):

– \(G'\) is commutative,

– the special fiber \(G'_0 = G_s\) is smooth over \(\kappa(s)\),

– for every integer \(\nu>0\), \({}_{p^\nu}G'\) is finite over \(S'\) and \({}_{p^\nu}H'\) is separated over \(S'\).

Under these conditions, u is an immersion.

It suffices to note that in c), the condition that \({}_pG'_0\) be finite over \(S'\) implies that \({}_pG'_0\) is finite over \(\kappa(s)\), which already implies that \(G'_0 \otimes_{\kappa(s)} \overline{\kappa(s)}\) has no subgroup isomorphic to the additive group, so that we are under the hypotheses of 7.5.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 7.10

Examples of M. Raynaud (XVI 1.1 a) and b)) show that in c) one can drop neither the hypothesis that the \({}_{p^\nu}G'\) be finite over \(S'\), nor the one that the \({}_{p^\nu}H'\) be separated over \(S'\).

We now want conditions ensuring that u is a closed immersion. We therefore keep the hypotheses preceding 7.9, but no longer assuming u surjective (only u an isomorphism on the generic fibers). We already know that \(u : G \to H\) is an open immersion, hence so is \(u_0 : G_0 \to H_0\), whose image therefore contains the connected component of the neutral element \((H_0)^0\). Note that since \(G_0\) is smooth over k and "without additive component" over the algebraic closure of k, the same holds for \(H_0\). Now we have the following

blocks.title: Lemme
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.11

Let H be a commutative algebraic group over a field k, such that \(H \otimes_k \overline{k}\) contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). Let \(n = \deg H/H^0\); then the homomorphism \[ {}_nH \longrightarrow H/H^0 \] is surjective.
Indeed, we may assume k algebraically closed, and then this follows from the well-known fact that \(H^0(k)\) is a divisible group.

Suppose now (returning to our situation \(u : G \to H\)) that the \({}_nG\) (\(n>0\)) are proper over S, and the \({}_nH\) are separated over S. Note on the other hand that the \({}_nH\) are flat over S. Indeed, it suffices to see this at the points above s; we are then reduced to proving that \(n\cdot\mathrm{id}_H\) is flat at the points above s, and for this we are reduced to checking that \(n\cdot\mathrm{id}_{H_0}\) is flat, which is equivalent (as we have already noted) to the fact that \((H_0)^0\) is smooth40 over k and has no \(\mathbb{G}_a\) component over the algebraic closure of k. Since \((H_0)^0 = (G_0)^0\), this follows from the analogous hypothesis made on \(G_0\).

On the other hand, the \({}_nH\) are separated over S since H is, so the morphism \({}_nG \to {}_nH\) is proper, hence its image is closed. Since this image contains the generic fiber of \({}_nH\) (because \(u_{s'} : G_{s'} \to H_{s'}\) is an isomorphism) and \({}_nH\) is flat over S, hence identical to the closure of its generic fiber, it follows that \({}_nG \to {}_nH\) is surjective, so \({}_nG_0 \to {}_nH_0\) is surjective. Remembering that \(G_0 \supset (H_0)^0\) and applying 7.11, one finds that \(G_0 \to H_0\) is surjective, so u is surjective. One thus obtains:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.12

With the notation of 7.9, suppose that conditions a) and b) are satisfied, as well as condition c′) (stronger than c)), obtained by requiring that for every integer \(n>0\) (not only of the form \(p^\nu\)), \({}_nG'\) be finite over \(S'\) and \({}_nH'\) separated over \(S'\). Under these conditions, u is a closed immersion.

blocks.title: Remarque
blocks.html:
Remark 7.13

a) One easily checks that the separation hypothesis made on the \({}_nH\) in fact implies that H is separated over S. This is moreover formally contained in 7.12 by taking for G the unit S-group. Note also that when S is locally noetherian, one may in 7.9 merely assume H locally of finite type over S (instead of: of finite type over S), the proof given applying as is; for 7.12 one will assume in addition that the fibers of H are of finite type.

b) Using 7.12, it is not hard to prove that if \(u : G \to H\) is a monomorphism of S-group preschemes of finite presentation, with G diagonalizable (or more generally, "of multiplicative type") and H separated over S, then u is a closed immersion, — which constitutes a satisfactory generalization of the first conclusion stated in 5.7. When G is smooth over S, it suffices to apply 7.12, and the general case reduces easily to that one. When H is no longer assumed separated over S, one can still show that u is an immersion; in the case where G is a torus, this fact also follows, moreover, from what comes next.

c) When in 7.9 G is assumed to have connected fibers, one may in condition 7.9 c) drop the hypothesis that the \({}_{p^\nu}H'\) be separated over \(S'\). Indeed, with the reductions made in the proof, one may assume H flat over S and u bijective, hence H with connected fibers, hence H separated over S by virtue of Raynaud's theorem (VIB 5.5).41
